summary: Gruber reviews Meta Connect 2024, noting Zuckerberg's confident solo keynote performance and the key announcements: the $300 Quest 3S headset, limited-edition translucent Ray-Ban smart glasses, and the Orion AR glasses prototype. He argues that while Orion is genuinely interesting as a concept, the 'almost normal glasses' framing is generous given their chunky, conspicuous appearance and $10,000-per-unit manufacturing cost. He frames the broader narrative as an exciting competitive volley between Meta and Apple over the future of spatial computing, with Meta effectively conceding that VR headsets aren't the endgame by pivoting toward AR glasses.
key_points: Zuckerberg's solo 45-minute keynote was punchy and effective, avoiding the typical parade of VPs that slows corporate presentations
The $300 Quest 3S is over 10x cheaper than Vision Pro, and while less capable, may be sufficient for what people actually want from headsets right now
Orion AR glasses are genuinely interesting but The Verge's 'almost normal' description stretches credulity — they're conspicuously chunky with glowing prismatic lenses
Meta's pivot from metaverse VR to AR glasses implicitly validates Apple's dismissal of the metaverse concept while also suggesting VR headsets are a dead end
The competitive back-and-forth between Apple and Meta signals the exciting, uncertain early years of a new device category
money_quotes: Nothing slows an on-stage keynote down more than a parade of VPs.

Vision Pro might be more than 10× more capable than Quest 3S, but I'm not sure it's 10× better for just playing games and watching movies, which might be the only things people want to do with headsets at the moment.
That's stretching the meaning of 'almost' to a breaking point.
They look orthopedic, like glasses intended to aid people whose vision is so low they're legally blind.
Unlike going out in public wearing a VR headset, you'd just look like a nerd, not a jackass.

Meta drops the 'metaverse' thing and previews Orion, effectively declaring that they think VR headsets are the wrong thing to build to create the product that defines the next breakthrough step change in personal computing.
Big bold ideas get tried out, and most of them wind up as dead ends to abandon.
key_insight: Meta's pivot from metaverse VR headsets to AR glasses represents a tacit admission that Apple was right to dismiss the metaverse, while the competitive volleying between the two companies signals the exciting uncertainty of a nascent device category.
